examples.title: Unified Process Phase Execution
examples.description: Pseudocode demonstrating how UP phases execute iteratively with workflows nested within each iteration.
examples.code:
PROCEDURE ExecuteUnifiedProcess()
 project = InitializeProject()
 
 // Inception Phase
 iterationCount = 1
 WHILE NOT InceptionComplete(project)
 ExecuteWorkflows(iterationCount, "Inception")
 iterationCount = iterationCount + 1
 END WHILE
 artifacts = CreateInceptionArtifacts()
 
 // Elaboration Phase
 iterationCount = 1
 WHILE NOT ArchitectureStable(project)
 ExecuteWorkflows(iterationCount, "Elaboration")
 iterationCount = iterationCount + 1
 END WHILE
 artifacts = CreateElaborationArtifacts()
 
 // Construction Phase
 iterationCount = 1
 WHILE NOT SystemComplete(project)
 increment = ExecuteWorkflows(iterationCount, "Construction")
 DeliverIncremental(increment)
 iterationCount = iterationCount + 1
 END WHILE
 
 // Transition Phase
 iterationCount = 1
 WHILE NOT StakeholdersAccepted(project)
 ExecuteWorkflows(iterationCount, "Transition")
 iterationCount = iterationCount + 1
 END WHILE
 
END PROCEDURE

PROCEDURE ExecuteWorkflows(iterationNum, phase)
 // Each iteration executes all core workflows with varying emphasis
 IF phase == "Inception" THEN
 BusinessModeling()
 Requirements()
 ELSIF phase == "Elaboration" THEN
 BusinessModeling()
 Requirements()
 AnalysisAndDesign()
 Implementation()
 ELSIF phase == "Construction" THEN
 Requirements()
 AnalysisAndDesign()
 Implementation()
 Test()
 ELSIF phase == "Transition" THEN
 Test()
 Deployment()
 END IF
 ConfigurationManagement()
 ProjectManagement()
END PROCEDURE
examples.explanation: This pseudocode illustrates the iterative nature of UP where each phase may contain multiple iterations, and each iteration executes workflows appropriate to that phase. The process continues until phase completion criteria are met.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n × m) where n = number of iterations, m = number of workflows per iteration
examples.spaceComplexity: O(k) where k = number of project artifacts

examples.title: UP Risk-Driven Iteration Planning
examples.description: Pseudocode showing how risk assessment drives iteration planning in UP.
examples.code:
PROCEDURE PlanIterations(project)
 risks = IdentifyRisks(project)
 sortedRisks = SortByPriority(risks) // Highest risk first
 
 iterationPlan = []
 currentIteration = 1
 
 WHILE sortedRisks NOT empty AND currentIteration <= maxIterations
 iterationScope = []
 
 // Address top risks in current iteration
 FOR each risk IN sortedRisks
 IF risk.severity == HIGH AND NOT risk.addressed THEN
 iterationScope = iterationScope + risk.mitigationActivities
 risk.addressed = TRUE
 END IF
 END FOR
 
 // Add incremental feature development
 remainingFeatures = GetUndevelopedFeatures(project)
 iterationScope = iterationScope + SelectFeatures(remainingFeatures, iterationCapacity)
 
 // Create iteration plan
 iterationPlan.append(CreateIteration(currentIteration, iterationScope))
 sortedRisks = UpdateRiskStatus(sortedRisks)
 currentIteration = currentIteration + 1
 END WHILE
 
 RETURN iterationPlan
END PROCEDURE

PROCEDURE ExecuteIteration(iteration)
 // Standard iteration workflow
 requirements = GatherRequirements(iteration.scope)
 design = CreateDesign(requirements)
 implementation = ImplementComponents(design)
 testResults = ExecuteTests(implementation)
 
 IF testResults.passed THEN
 DeliverIncremental(implementation)
 UpdateProjectBaseline()
 ELSE
 RefineAndRetry(iteration)
 END IF
END PROCEDURE
examples.explanation: This code demonstrates UP's risk-driven approach. High-priority risks are addressed in early iterations before significant development investment. Each iteration produces a tested, deliverable increment, and risk status is continuously updated.
examples.timeComplexity: O(r log r + i × w) where r = number of risks, i = iterations, w = workflows per iteration
examples.spaceComplexity: O(r + i) where r = risk list size, i = iteration plan size

examples.title: Process Tailoring Decision Logic
examples.description: Pseudocode demonstrating how UP can be tailored based on project characteristics.
examples.code:
PROCEDURE TailorUPForProject(projectCharacteristics)
 standardWorkflows = ["BusinessModeling", "Requirements", "AnalysisAndDesign",
 "Implementation", "Test", "Deployment",
 "ConfigurationManagement", "ProjectManagement", "Environment"]
 
 selectedWorkflows = []
 
 // Project size assessment
 IF projectCharacteristics.teamSize < 5 THEN
 // Small project: minimize overhead
 selectedWorkflows = RemoveFromList(standardWorkflows, "Environment")
 selectedWorkflows = CombineWorkflows(selectedWorkflows, "BusinessModeling", "Requirements")
 ELSE
 selectedWorkflows = standardWorkflows
 END IF
 
 // Criticality assessment
 IF projectCharacteristics.criticality == HIGH THEN
 // Enhance testing and configuration
 selectedWorkflows = AddToList(selectedWorkflows, "EnhancedTesting")
 selectedWorkflows = AddToList(selectedWorkflows, "RigorousCM")
 END IF
 
 // Experience assessment
 IF projectCharacteristics.teamExperience == HIGH THEN
 // Reduce documentation overhead
 selectedWorkflows = ReduceArtifactDetail(selectedWorkflows)
 END IF
 
 // Generate tailored process plan
 processPlan = CreateProcessPlan(selectedWorkflows, projectCharacteristics)
 
 RETURN processPlan
END PROCEDURE
examples.explanation: This pseudocode shows how UP can be customized. For small teams, overhead-reducing measures like combining workflows and minimizing environment setup are applied. For critical systems, enhanced testing and configuration management are added. Team experience also influences the level of formality required.
examples.timeComplexity: O(w × c) where w = number of workflows, c = number of tailoring criteria
examples.spaceComplexity: O(w) where w = selected workflow count
